original_abstract: In this study, we utilize first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory to explore the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer MoS$_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. Unlike graphene, where mobility significantly deteriorates when patterned into nanoribbons, the mobility of armchair MoS$_2$ nanoribbons remains comparable to that of the two-dimensional sheet, despite exhibiting oscillations dependent on ribbon width. Strikingly, we observe a reversal in room-temperature transport polarity: while the MoS$_2$ sheet exhibits hole and electron mobilities of 200.52 and 72.16 cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, respectively, a 4 nm nanoribbon switches these values to 49.72 (hole) and 190.89 (electron) cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$. This robust mobility and the unexpected polarity reversal are driven by the unique characteristics of the inherent edge states in MoS$_2$ nanoribbons. Our findings suggest that combining width confinement with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for tailoring and enhancing the transport properties of MoS$_2$-based nanostructures.
humanized_abstract: We investigate the intrinsic carrier mobility (μ) in monolayer MoS2 sheets and nanoribbons by using first-principle calculations combined with deformation potential theory. We observe a prominent feature of mobility oscillations with width, which still maintains its major component in a comparable level with the 2D sheet for armchair MoS2 nanoribbons. Moreover, there is also a reversal of transport polarity at room temperature, while the 2D MoS2 sheet always behaves as a p-type material with a large hole mobility of 200.52 cm2 V-1 s-1 and a small electron mobility of 72.16 cm2 V-1 s-1, a 4 nm wide armchair nanoribbon is transformed into an n-type material at room temperature with a hole mobility of 49.72 cm2 V-1 s-1 and an electron mobility of 190.89 cm2 V-1 s-1. Our findings also indicate that the width confinement combined with adequate edge engineering would have a promising future in tailoring the carrier transport behavior in MoS2-based materials.

document.output: We investigate the intrinsic carrier mobility (μ) in monolayer MoS2 sheets and nanoribbons by using first-principle calculations combined with deformation potential theory. We observe a prominent feature of mobility oscillations with width, which still maintains its major component in a comparable level with the 2D sheet for armchair MoS2 nanoribbons. Moreover, there is also a reversal of transport polarity at room temperature, while the 2D MoS2 sheet always behaves as a p-type material with a large hole mobility of 200.52 cm2 V-1 s-1 and a small electron mobility of 72.16 cm2 V-1 s-1, a 4 nm wide armchair nanoribbon is transformed into an n-type material at room temperature with a hole mobility of 49.72 cm2 V-1 s-1 and an electron mobility of 190.89 cm2 V-1 s-1. Our findings also indicate that the width confinement combined with adequate edge engineering would have a promising future in tailoring the carrier transport behavior in MoS2-based materials.
document.input: In this study, we utilize first-principles calculations and deformation potential theory to explore the intrinsic carrier mobility ($\mu$) of monolayer MoS$_2$ sheets and nanoribbons. Unlike graphene, where mobility significantly deteriorates when patterned into nanoribbons, the mobility of armchair MoS$_2$ nanoribbons remains comparable to that of the two-dimensional sheet, despite exhibiting oscillations dependent on ribbon width. Strikingly, we observe a reversal in room-temperature transport polarity: while the MoS$_2$ sheet exhibits hole and electron mobilities of 200.52 and 72.16 cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$, respectively, a 4 nm nanoribbon switches these values to 49.72 (hole) and 190.89 (electron) cm$^2$V$^{-1}$s$^{-1}$. This robust mobility and the unexpected polarity reversal are driven by the unique characteristics of the inherent edge states in MoS$_2$ nanoribbons. Our findings suggest that combining width confinement with edge engineering offers a powerful strategy for tailoring and enhancing the transport properties of MoS$_2$-based nanostructures.
